narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 68.8% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize students with stronger projected outcomes. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, particularly your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure—it's notably lower than the average of about 6 for those who passed. Your Year 1 decile of 4 played a close second role, similarly signaling weaker early performance compared to the typical 6 seen among successful bar takers, reinforcing the model's concerns about your trajectory. Even your upper-middle family income bracket, while common among passers (representing nearly half of them), contributed toward the failure prediction in your case, likely because the model associates it with certain patterns that didn't align favorably here. Your LSAT score of 36 and undergraduate GPA of 3.20 were close to averages for those who passed (37.27 and 3.24, respectively), so they had less sway, and your choice of full-time enrollment matched the norm for admittees. Overall, these top influences—led by your later-year grades—painted a picture riskier than the profiles of accepted students, who typically show higher decile rankings across the board. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your solid undergraduate record and LSAT performance suggesting real potential. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push closer to or above 37, or bolstering your recent grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve those decile standings. Building a stronger narrative around your full-time commitment and background could also help in reapplying or appealing to schools with holistic reviews. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting these areas—reach out to advisors for personalized strategies, and know that one model's view doesn't define your path to law. With targeted improvements, you could shift that prediction dramatically in your favor next time.
